title: Electronic mechanism of sub-100-fs demagnetization induced by a femtosecond light pulse
authors: Konrad J. Kapcia, Victor Tkachenko, Flavio Capotondi, Alexander Lichtenstein, Serguei Molodtsov, Przemysław Piekarz, Beata Ziaja
abstract: A quantitative understanding of the processes that trigger light-induced demagnetization on ultrashort timescales is crucial for achieving an ultrafast, radiation-controlled magnetic response in materials. This milestone is essential for developing next-generation magnetic storage devices and ultrafast magnetic switches. In this theoretical study, we investigated demagnetization triggered in a single magnetic domain by light pulses ranging from a few to a few tens of femtoseconds in duration, with photon energies spanning the optical and X-ray regimes, under strongly non-equilibrium conditions. We predicted a loss of magnetization in the sub-100-fs range in all cases, primarily due to the excitation of the electronic system and the subsequent redistribution of electrons within the magneto-sensitive band. The considered timescales were too short for phonon-mediated processes or inter-site Heisenberg exchange processes to contribute significantly. These findings pave the way for highly accurate, radiation-driven magnetization control in magnetic materials at sub-100-femtosecond timescales with potential practical applications.
summary: This theoretical study demonstrates that demagnetization in Co and Ni occurs in less than 100 femtoseconds due to electronic redistribution in the 3d band. The mechanism is shown to be universal across optical and X-ray regimes, provided the photon energy is sufficient to excite the electronic system. These findings are critical for developing next-generation ultrafast magnetic storage and switching technologies.
main_problem: Identifying the specific physical processes that trigger ultrafast (sub-100 fs) radiation-induced demagnetization in magnetic materials across optical to X-ray photon energies.
main_result: Sub-100-fs demagnetization is driven by electronic excitation and subsequent electron redistribution within the 3d band, a mechanism that remains universal across different photon energies.
method: The study uses the XSPIN simulation code, which combines VASP-calculated electronic structures with a Monte Carlo approach for high-energy electrons and a transient Fermi-Dirac distribution for low-energy electrons.
model_or_system: The system consists of single magnetic domains of bulk fcc Nickel (Ni) and Cobalt (Co) under strongly non-equilibrium conditions following femtosecond light pulses.
key_observables: Spin-resolved number of deep-shell holes, spin-resolved number of low-energy electrons (below 15 eV), transient electronic temperature, and normalized magnetization (m/m0).
important_parameters: Photon energies from 2 eV to 10,000 eV; pulse durations of 2 fs and 7/0 fs FWHM; absorbed dose of 0.93 eV/atom.
main_assumptions: The band structure remains static (no nuclei movement); phonon-mediated processes and inter-site Heisenberg exchange are negligible on these timescales; the electronic temperature and chemical potential are common for both spin populations.
figures_summary: Figure 1 compares Co under 2 eV and 1000 eV irradiation, showing the evolution of spin-resolved holes, low-energy electrons, electronic temperature, and normalized magnetization.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of ultrafast demagnetization, describes the XSPIN modeling framework and electronic structure calculations, presents results for various photon energies and pulse durations, and discusses the implications of electron cascading and experimental convolution effects.
why_interesting: The study explores non-equilibrium dynamics and the relaxation of highly excited electronic systems, which is relevant to understanding how ultrafast energy deposition drives transient changes in many-body electronic states.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Transient electronic and magnetic properties of Co predicted by XSPIN in an single magnetic domain under optical irradiation with 2 eV pulse or X-ray irradiation with 1000 eV pulse (as labeled), both of 70 fs FWHM duration. (a) number of deep-shell holes produced (per atom; spin-resolved), (b) number of low-energy electrons per atom (with energy below the cutoff of 15 eV, spin-resolved), (c) electronic temperature, (d) magnetization (normalized to its initial value m0 before the pulse), all plotted as a function of time. Temporal profile of the pulse triggering the dynamics is schematically depicted.

Figure 2. Transient electronic and magnetic properties of Co predicted by XSPIN in a single magnetic domain under optical irradiation with 2 eV pulse or X-ray irradiation with 1000 eV pulse (as labeled), both of 2 fs FWHM duration. Other simulation parameters are as in Fig. 1.

Figure 3. Transient electronic and magnetic properties of Ni predicted by XSPIN in a single magnetic domain under optical irradiation with 2 eV pulse or X-ray irradiation with 1000 eV pulse (as labeled), both of 70 fs FWHM duration. Other simulation parameters are as in Fig. 1.

Figure 4. Transient electronic and magnetic properties of Ni predicted by XSPIN in a single magnetic domain under optical irradiation with 2 eV pulse or X-ray irradiation with 1000 eV pulse (as labeled), both of 2 fs FWHM duration. Other simulation parameters are as in Fig. 1.
